agent1_snippets.title: Antiparasitic Drugs -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: The drug of choice to treat malaria is dependent on the Plasmodium species, the geographic region of the infecting species, and the severity of the patient’s infection. [2] Chloroquine is the drug of choice to treat uncomplicated malaria caused by all Plasmodium species ( P. vivax, P. malariae, P. ovale, and P. knowlesi ) except P. falciparum , which has become increasingly resistant. [3] Amodiaquine (discontinued in USA) can be used in combination with artesunate to treat chloroquine-resistant uncomplicated P. falciparum. [3] An atovaquone-proguanil combination or an artemether-lumefantrine combination may also be a choice for first-line treatment for chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum . In pregnancy, oral quinine is indicated, and parenteral quinine(discontinued in USA) is useful for the treatment of severe malaria. Primaquine, mefloquine, atovaquone-proguanil, and doxycycline are indicated for chemoprophylaxis. [3] Antibabesial agents:

agent1_snippets.content: In regions where P. vivax is chloroquine resistant the CDC offers three possible treatment options: atovaquone-proguanil, quinine plus either tetracycline or doxycycline or mefloquine. Providers should select one of these three options to combine with primaquine.

agent1_snippets.title: Malaria -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Treatment involves combination therapy targeting both the hepatic and erythrocytic forms. [17] The chief antimalarials are chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, primaquine, artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT), and atovaquone-proguanil. Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine are synthetic forms of quinine. [18] [19] They disrupt the erythrocytic stage by interfering with parasitic hemoglobin metabolism and increasing intracellular pH. [18] [19] They generally require two days of treatment, allowing for better tolerance and shorter admissions. [2] However, chloroquine may enhance gametogenesis, contributing to resistance, which is a concern, particularly in South Asia. [17] Primaquine is a hypnozointocidal agent added for P. vivax or P. ovale infection for the eradication of liver parasites and the prevention of dormancy and relapse. [2] [20]

agent1_snippets.title: Plasmodium falciparum Malaria -- Treatment / Management -- Uncomplicated Falciparum Malaria
agent1_snippets.content: Uncomplicated falciparum malaria should be treated with one of the artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACT). Artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) is highly effective due to its effect on a broader range of parasite life cycles, causing faster parasite clearance and is therefore considered a drug of choice for uncomplicated malaria. [24] The duration of ACT treatment is 3 days. ACTs are also recommended for pregnant women in the second and third trimester but should be used during the first trimester only if other treatment options are not available. During the first trimester, a combination of quinine and clindamycin should be prescribed. In low transmission areas, gametocytocidal therapy (e.g., primaquine) is added to ACTs to reduce transmission potential (except pregnant women, infants younger than 6 months and women breastfeeding infants younger than 6 months). [18]

agent1_snippets.title: Plasmodium falciparum Malaria -- Treatment / Management -- Severe Falciparum Malaria
agent1_snippets.content: Intravenous artesunate is currently not approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) or commercially available in the U.S. but is available from CDC under an expanded-access investigational drug (IND) protocol. It is prepositioned at distribution sites throughout the USA and readily available after contacting CDC. Since severe malaria can progress rapidly, CDC offers guidance on oral treatment that can be used while waiting for IV artesunate to be delivered. They recommend interim treatment with oral artemether-lumefantrine (preferably), atovaquone-proguanil, quinine sulfate, or mefloquine. If a patient can not tolerate oral medications, administration after an antiemetic or via nasogastric tube should be considered.

agent1_snippets.content: Primaquine is contraindicated in pregnant and G6PD deficient patients due to fetal teratogenicity and hemolytic reaction (will see bite cells and Heinz bodies on blood smear), respectively. [3] Artemisinins are active against all parasite lifecycle stages. [2] Atovaquone targets the cellular electron transport chain inhibiting ATP production; proguanil enhances atovaquone’s effect by sensitizing parasitic mitochondria. [21] Atovaquone-proguanil is active against the erythrocytic and extraerythrocytic forms. agent1_snippets.content: Contraindications for both quinine and quinidine include thrombocytopenia, thrombocytopenic purpura, myasthenia gravis, any heart block greater than first degree, QT prolongation, or in combination with medications that can also cause QT prolongation. [18]

agent1_snippets.title: Plasmodium falciparum Malaria -- Treatment / Management -- Severe Falciparum Malaria
agent1_snippets.content: If artesunate is not available, the recommended drug is artemether in preference to quinine. After the complete course of a 24 hour intravenous artesunate, a regimen of a follow-up drug should be completed. A full 3-day regimen of ACT is recommended. In returning traveler, the follow-on antimalarial medication needs to be other than the antimalarial medication taken for prophylaxis. [18]

agent1_snippets.title: Antiparasitic Drugs -- Administration -- Antiprotozoal Agents
agent1_snippets.content: Severe or complicated P. falciparum infection is treated using intravenous (IV) artesunate 2.4 mg/kg every 12 hours for one day, then once a day for two additional days. Subsequent to this regimen, use doxycycline or clindamycin orally for 7 days, or artemether/lumefantrine, atovaquone/proguanil, or full mefloquine treatment. Another choice of drug is 10 mg/kg IV quinidine gluconate administered over 1 to 2 hours, followed by 0.02 mg/kg/min. Alternatively, 3.2 mg/kg artemether can be administered intramuscularly (IM), then 1.6 mg/kg/d is administered, followed by a 7-day oral course of clindamycin or doxycycline. Another alternative is 20 mg/kg intravenous quinine dihydrochloride then 10 mg/kg every 8 hours. During IV administration of quinine, monitor the patients’ cardiac activity.

agent1_snippets.title: Antimalarial Medications -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: When treating patients suspected of having malaria, it is important that treatment does not start until there is a definitive diagnosis of malaria. To guide malaria treatment appropriately, it is important to identify three factors: (1) the infecting Plasmodium species, (2) the clinical status of the patient, and (3) the drug susceptibility of the infecting parasites (the geographic area from where the infection was acquired from and any previous antimalarial medications). The obvious exception is in waiting for confirmation to treat suspected malaria if the patient shows signs of severe malaria, and clinical suspicion for malaria is high. [1] [2] [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Plasmodium vivax Malaria -- Pearls and Other Issues
agent1_snippets.content: Plasmodium vivax is the most widespread malaria species with predominance in Latin America and Southeast Asia The blood smear is the gold standard diagnostic tool for malaria, but in resource-limited clinical settings, rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) are a reasonable alternative Treatment decisions need to account for the severity of disease and local patterns of drug resistance The uncomplicated disease is treatable with chloroquine or hydroxychloroquine in combination with primaquine In the setting of severe disease outside of the United States, IV artesunate is the treatment of choice In the United States severe vivax malaria can be treated with intravenous quinidine plus tetracycline, or doxycycline, or clindamycin (artesunate is available in the United States as an investigational new drug (IND) protocol # 76,725 via the CDC)

agent1_snippets.title: Malaria Prophylaxis -- Issues of Concern -- Special Populations
agent1_snippets.content: Pregnant travelers should generally be counseled to avoid travel to malaria-endemic areas until after delivery as malaria increases the risk for prematurity, spontaneous abortion, and stillbirth. Malaria may also be more severe in pregnant patients.  In situations where deferring travel is not possible, mefloquine, chloroquine, and hydroxychloroquine are safe for pregnancy when accounting for the usual contraindications and resistance patterns. Doxycycline is contraindicated in pregnant women due to concerns for fetal dental discoloration and bone growth inhibition. Atovaquone-proguanil has not been well-studied in pregnant women and is generally not prescribed in this population.  Primaquine should also not be used in pregnant travelers due to concerns about fetal G6PD deficiency.

agent1_snippets.title: Plasmodium vivax Malaria -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Chloroquine remains the mainstay of treatment for uncomplicated vivax malaria. Exceptions to this include infection acquired in areas of chloroquine resistance, primarily Papua New Guinea and Indonesia [8] . According to the CDC appropriate treatment of chloroquine-sensitive uncomplicated vivax malaria includes Chloroquine phosphate or hydroxychloroquine plus primaquine. Dosing is listed below. Chloroquine phosphate: 600 mg base (=1,000 mg salt) orally immediately, followed by 300 mg base (=500 mg salt) orally at 6, 24, and 48 hours Total dose: 1,500 mg base (=2,500 mg salt) OR Hydroxychloroquine: 620 mg base (=800 mg salt) orally immediately, followed by 310 mg base (=400 mg salt) orally at 6, 24, and 48 hours Primaquine phosphate: 30 mg base orally once daily for 14 days

agent1_snippets.title: Plasmodium falciparum Malaria -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: The treatment should be guided by three main factors: the infecting Plasmodium species, the clinical status of the patient, and drug susceptibility of infecting Plasmodium , determined by the geographic region where the infection was acquired. Chloroquine sensitivity can be expected if the infection was acquired in Central America west of Panama Canal, Haiti, and the Dominican Republic. In a case when the diagnosis is strongly suspected, and it cannot be confirmed, or when there is no species determination possible, given the global spread of P. falciparum resistant to chloroquine, the clinician should opt for a treatment option effective against chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum . CDC recommends making additional blood smears in infections with P. falciparum after initiating the treatment, to confirm adequate parasitological response (decrease in density) to treatment.

agent1_snippets.title: Chloroquine -- Contraindications
agent1_snippets.content: Chloroquine has been proven safe to use during pregnancy and in children. Chloroquine has few but serious contraindications. There are reports of cases with death relating to chloroquine administered to patients with porphyria cutaneous tarda. [14] Chloroquine should not be used in patients with retinal or vision changes unless treating acute malaria. Chloroquine is also contraindicated in patients who suffer from a known hydroxychloroquine sensitivity.

agent1_snippets.title: Antimalarial Medications -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: By identifying the infecting Plasmodium species, the healthcare practitioner can identify which infections will progress to severe manifestations and which will not. Also, some infections can remain dormant in the liver as hypnozoites and can lead to a relapse. The clinical status of a patient can fall into two main categories: uncomplicated malaria or severe malaria. The main difference in treatment is that uncomplicated malaria is treated with oral antimalarials, while severe malaria gets treated with parenteral antimalarials. Last, healthcare practitioners can select an appropriate treatment course by determining the drug susceptibility of the infecting Plasmodium species. Practitioners can do this by looking at where the patient was when they acquired the infection and if they have received any previous treatment with antimalarials.

agent1_snippets.title: Malaria Prophylaxis -- Issues of Concern -- Special Populations
agent1_snippets.content: Children Atovaquone-proguanil is an option for children over 5 kg.  Mefloquine, chloroquine, and primaquine can be prescribed to children, taking into account the usual contraindications and resistance patterns. Children under the age of 8 should not receive doxycycline due to concerns of dental discoloration. Pediatric weight-based doses should not exceed adult doses and may be tolerated best when mixed with something sweet to overcome the bitter taste. The medication should also be taken on a full stomach to reduce the risk of vomiting or other GI upset. Overdose of antimalarials, especially chloroquine, can be fatal to children - childproof bottles and regular safety measures are essential.

agent1_snippets.title: Antimalarial Medications -- Administration
agent1_snippets.content: Quinine gluconate, used as the only IV option for severe malaria treatment, is given as a loading dose of 10mg/kg IV over one to two hours, followed by a continuous infusion of 0.02 mg/kg/minute for at least 24 hours. Patients may alternatively receive a 24 mg/kg loading dose infused over four hours, followed by 12 mg/kg over four hours dosed every eight hours. IV quinidine should continue until the parasite density is less than 1% and the patient can tolerate oral medications. After discontinuation of IV quinine, the patient should switch to oral quinine for the remainder of treatment. Renal adjustments are not necessary when calculating loading doses of IV quinidine gluconate. If a patient has a creatinine clearance less than or equal to 1 mL/minute, the maintenance dose should be reduced by 25%. The addition of doxycycline, tetracycline, or clindamycin to intravenous (IV) quinidine is also given as a 7-day course. [11]

agent1_snippets.title: Antimalarial Medications -- Administration
agent1_snippets.content: Mefloquine is administered as 750 mg by initial mouth dose followed by 500 mg by mouth, given 6 to 12 hours after the initial dose. Mefloquine requires administration with food and at least 8 ounces of water. An additional full dose is necessary if vomiting occurs within 30 minutes after the dose; if it occurs within 30 to 60 minutes after the dose, an additional half-dose should be given. This agent can be useful as a third-line option for the treatment of malaria caused by chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum or a first-line option for the treatment of uncomplicated chloroquine-resistant P. vivax . [10]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 29
agent2_snippets.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial therapy, which is largely delivered in oral tablet form. The antimalarial class includes a wide range of agents such as acedapsone; artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg tablets; atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg tablets and the lower‑strength atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg tablets that combine a mitochondrial electron‑transport inhibitor with a dihydrofolate‑reductase antagonist; chloroquine phosphate tablets (250 mg and 500 mg); hydroxychloroquine sulfate tablets (100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg, 400 mg), both 4‑aminoquinoline compounds active against blood‑stage parasites; mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg tablets; primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg tablets targeting hepatic stages; pyrimethamine 25 mg tablets; and tafenoquine tablets (100 mg and 150 mg) that interfere with folate metabolism or mitochondrial function. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 30
agent2_snippets.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite responsible for malaria, is treated with a broad spectrum of antimalarial agents that are predominantly formulated as oral tablets. Combination tablets include artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg, atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg, and a lower‑strength atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg. Single‑agent antimalarial tablets comprise chloroquine phosphate (250 mg and 500 mg), hydroxychloroquine sulfate (100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg, 400 mg), mefloquine hydrochloride (250 mg), primaquine phosphate (26.3 mg), pyrimethamine (25 mg), sulfadoxine, tafenoquine (100 mg and 150 mg), and the identified agent acedapsone. Additional antimalarial products mentioned are chinidin and tafenoquine. All of these medications share the tablet dosage form, providing a standardized route of administration that facilitates dosing flexibility and patient adherence. The diversity of chemical structures and mechanisms of action among these tablets enables clinicians to tailor malaria prophylaxis and therapy to individual patient needs, including considerations of resistance patterns, severity of infection, and safety profiles. The tablet dosage form is also employed across other therapeutic classes, exemplified by griseofulvin 500 mg (an antifungal) and hydrocortisone tablets (5 mg, 10 mg, 20 mg), underscoring the widespread utility of oral tablets in modern pharmacotherapy.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
agent2_snippets.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial therapy. A wide range of antimalarial agents are formulated as oral tablets, providing convenient dosing and supporting adherence to treatment regimens. Tablet formulations include artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg, atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg, atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg, chloroquine phosphate 250 mg and 500 mg, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg and 400 mg, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg, pyrimethamine 25 mg, tafenoquine 100 mg and 150 mg, as well as the natural product chinidin and sulfadoxin. Acedapsone is classified as an antimalarial and is used for treatment and prevention of Plasmodium infection. Although trimethoprim + sulfamethoxazole is occasionally listed among antimalarials, it is primarily an antibacterial combination and represents a misclassification. In addition to antimalarials, other therapeutic agents are available as oral tablets, such as the antifungal griseofulvin 500 mg and the corticosteroids hydrocortisone 5 mg, 10 mg and 20 mg, illustrating the broad use of the tablet dosage form across drug classes. Recognizing the specific agents, their tablet strengths, mechanisms of action, and appropriate indications is essential for optimal drug selection, dosing, management of malaria, and minimization of drug‑resistance risk.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 915
agent2_snippets.content: Artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg oral tablet, chloroquine phosphate 500 mg oral tablet, griseofulvin 500 mg oral tablet, hydrocortisone 5 mg oral tablet, hydrocortisone 10 mg oral tablet, hydrocortisone 20 mg oral tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 100 mg oral tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 300 mg oral tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 400 mg oral tablet, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg oral tablet, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg oral tablet, and pyrimethamine 25 mg oral tablet are all formulated as tablets for oral administration. This shared dose form provides a familiar and convenient vehicle for a diverse group of therapeutic agents—including antimalarials, an antifungal, a corticosteroid, and an antiparasitic—facilitating prescribing, simplifying medication management, and supporting patient adherence. The specific milligram strengths listed for each product reflect the dosing requirements of their respective indications, allowing precise titration and appropriate therapeutic effect while maintaining the advantages of a tablet formulation.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 517
agent2_snippets.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial agents. The antimalarial drug class encompasses a diverse range of compounds, each with distinct formulations and therapeutic roles. Acedapson is classified as an antimalarial, as are the fixed‑dose oral tablet containing atovaquone 62.5 mg and proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg, and the quinoline‑derived agent chinidin. Chloroquine phosphate, commonly supplied as a 250 mg oral tablet, remains a classic antimalarial, while hydroxychloroquine sulfate is available in multiple tablet strengths (100 mg, 300 mg, and 400 mg) and is also employed for malaria treatment and prophylaxis. Sulfadoxine, traditionally used in combination with pyrimethamine, is another antimalarial included in this group. Additionally, mirincamycin—a macrolide‑type compound—has been identified as an antimalarial, and the combination of trimethoprim and sulfamethoxazole, although primarily an antibacterial regimen, possesses activity against Plasmodium and is therefore sometimes considered within the antimalarial spectrum. Collectively, these agents illustrate the breadth of pharmacologic strategies used to prevent and treat malaria, highlighting the importance of selecting appropriate therapy based on the specific Plasmodium species, resistance patterns, and patient factors.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 112
agent2_snippets.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is treated with a wide spectrum of antimalarial agents that are primarily formulated as oral tablets. Fixed‑dose combinations include artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg; atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil 25 mg and atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil 100 mg, both used for treatment and chemoprophylaxis. Chloroquine phosphate is available in 250 mg and 500 mg strengths, while hydroxychloroquine sulfate is marketed in 100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg and 400 mg tablets for chloroquine‑sensitive Plasmodium infections and for rheumatologic indications that retain antimalarial activity. Mefloquine hydrochloride is supplied as a 250 mg tablet, primaquine phosphate as a 26.3 mg tablet, pyrimethamine as a 25 mg tablet, and sulfadoxin (sulfadoxine) is used in combination regimens to enhance efficacy and limit resistance. Tafenoquine is provided in 100 mg and 150 mg tablets, representing newer single‑dose options for radical cure of hepatic stages. Additional antimalarial compounds include the peptide dermaseptin, the natural product chinidin, and the antibiotic mirincamycin, all of which possess activity against Plasmodium. Beyond antimalarials, other therapeutic agents are also available as tablets, such as griseofulvin 500 mg (an antifungal) and hydrocortisone 5 mg (a corticosteroid). Peptide‑based antimicrobials are represented by dermaseptin, which belongs to the class of cationic antimicrobial peptides; this class also includes magainin 1 peptide derived from the amphibian *Xenopus*, illustrating a hierarchical relationship among cationic, antimicrobial peptide families. Collectively, these agents demonstrate the breadth of chemical structures, dosage forms, and therapeutic indications encompassed by the antimalarial category and related pharmacologic classes, underscoring their central role in global malaria control, patient care, and broader host‑defense strategies.

agent2_snippets.content: Atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg oral tablet, chloroquine phosphate 500 mg oral tablet, griseofulvin 500 mg oral tablet, hydrocortisone oral tablets (5 mg, 10 mg, and 20 mg), hydroxychloroquine sulfate 400 mg oral tablet, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg oral tablet, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg oral tablet, pyrimethamine 25 mg oral tablet, and tafenoquine oral tablets (100 mg and 150 mg) are all formulated as tablets for oral administration. Recognizing that each of these medications is delivered in tablet form is important for prescribing practices, dosing accuracy, and patient adherence, especially given the range of therapeutic classes represented—from antimalarials and antifungals to corticosteroids and antiprotozoal agents. The availability of hydrocortisone in multiple tablet strengths further illustrates the need to match specific dosage requirements to individual patient needs.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 273
agent2_snippets.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial therapy. Acedapson is classified as an antimalarial agent, and a broad range of medications share this classification. Specific antimalarial drugs include the combination tablet of artemether 20 mg and lumefantrine 120 mg, the combination tablet of atovaquone 62.5 mg and proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg, chinidin, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 200 mg tablets, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg tablets, mirincamycin, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg tablets, sulfadoxin, and tafenoquine 100 mg tablets. All of these agents are employed to prevent or treat Plasmodium infection, reflecting their shared role in malaria management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
agent2_snippets.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is treated with a diverse therapeutic class known as antimalarials. Antimalarial agents are formulated primarily as oral tablets, underscoring the importance of oral delivery for both prophylaxis and cure of malaria. The class includes the artemisinin‑based combination therapy artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg, fixed‑dose atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg and atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg, the 4‑aminoquinolines chloroquine phosphate (250 mg and 500 mg) and hydroxychloroquine sulfate (100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg, 400 mg), the quinoline‑methanol mefloquine hydrochloride (250 mg), the 8‑aminoquinolines primaquine phosphate (26.3 mg) and tafenoquine (100 mg, 150 mg), the dihydrofolate‑reductase inhibitor pyrimethamine (25 mg), the sulfonamide sulfadoxine, the natural product chinidin, and the investigational peptide dermaseptin. Dermaseptin belongs to the cationic antimicrobial peptide family and is conceptually linked to the amphibian magainin‑1 peptide, highlighting cationic peptides as a source of novel antimalarial compounds. Acedapson is also identified as an antimalarial agent within this class. In addition to antimalarials, oral tablet formulations listed include the antifungal griseofulvin 500 mg and corticosteroid hydrocortisone tablets (5 mg, 10 mg, 20 mg), illustrating the broader use of tablet dosage forms for infectious and inflammatory conditions. Recognizing the wide range of chemical families, dose strengths, and related peptide scaffolds within the antimalarial class supports comprehensive treatment strategies and informs ongoing drug development efforts.
